data.title: Asana CEO acquires shares worth ~$6.72M
data.description:
• Enterprise software company Asana (NYSE:ASAN) said in a filing that CEO Dustin Moskovitz acquired shares worth ~$6.72M.
  • Moskovitz acquired class A shares in three tranches - 159,025 shares at a weighted average purchase price per share of $14.07; 65,975 shares at $14.64; and 225,000 shares at $15.64.

data.title: Bill Ackman criticizes heavy use of leverage in the markets
data.description:
Bill Ackman, the billionaire investor and founder of Pershing Square Capital Management, questioned the use of highly leveraged ETFs and zero-day stock options, questioning their contribution to society and the economy.

“How have 3X leveraged ETFs and Zero Days to Expiration (0DTE) options advanced society or contributed to our economy?” he posted on X.com.

Leveraged funds allow investors to magnify their exposure to an asset or index. These funds are typically meant for short-term trading.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:TQQQ), for example, is up more than $2.3B this week so far, and is on track for the biggest weekly inflow in its history.

The zero-day stock options deal with 24-hour contracts, and some investors say they heighten volatility in the equity markets.

The SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), the S&P Index (SPX), and the S&P 500 Mini-SPX Index (XSP) list daily options.

“Investors and market commentators are reading too much into short-term market movements in rates, currencies and equities,” Ackman posted on X.com.

“I believe that it is much more likely that recent sharp moves in these asset classes is due to highly leveraged market participants being forced out of positions than due to fundamentals,” he said. “In other words, technical factors are driving the dramatic market moves.”

This has resulted in markets becoming highly unreliable as short-term impact indicators of policy changes, he said.

“I don’t understand how it is good for the world to allow investors in equities to operate with 10-1 leverage and investors in Treasurys and currencies to operate with 100-1 leverage,” he added. “When did we decide as a society to abandon the margin rules that were put in place to protect markets from this kind of volatility?”

Here are some of the top 3x leveraged ETFs, aside from TQQQ:

  • Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3x Shares (SOXL) – Average volume: 93M
  • Direxion Daily 20+ Year Treasury Bull 3X Shares (TMF) – Average volume: 139M
  • Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3X Shares (SPXL) – Average volume: 5.16M
  • ProShares UltraPro S&P500 (UPRO) – Average volume: 6.5M
  • Direxion Daily Technology Bull 3X Shares (TECL) – Average volume: 1.9M
  • Direxion Daily Financial Bull 3X Shares (FAS) – Average volume: 887.6k
  • Direxion Daily Small Cap Bull 3X Shares (TNA) – Average volume: 15M

data.title: Five Below tells vendors to turn back Chinese merchandise
data.description:
Amid escalating tensions in the U.S.-China trade war, Five Below (NASDAQ:FIVE) has halted shipments from China, according to a memo reviewed by Bloomberg.

The discount retailer, who imports as much as 70% of its merchandise from China, reportedly advised its shipping partner, A.P. Moller-Maersk, not to accept cargo waiting to be shipped out of China, and any containers that have been loaded onto ships must be unpacked and returned to the carrier. Bloomberg states it's uncertain if this applies to all Five Below (NASDAQ:FIVE) vendors or a subset.

With a majority of its merchandise priced at $5 or less, the company has very limited – if any – ability to offset tariffs through pricing strategies. At the current level, tariffs pose increased costs of 90% to 95% for Five Below, estimates Oppenheimer analyst Brian Nagel.

“We think Five Below has limited pricing power to mitigate tariffs as the value proposition is not resonating with consumers, and Five Below is already in the process of reprioritizing lower price point items,” said BofA Securities, adding that the company does not have “a clear path to sustainable positive comps.”

This recent development is weighing on shares, currently down 6% for a second day in the red.

data.title: Chinese electronics firm Anker raises prices on Amazon in response to tariffs: report
data.description:
China-based Anker has raised the prices of about 127 different products it sells on Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) by an average of 18% since last Thursday in response to tariffs, according to Reuters. 

Anker produces a wide range of consumer electronics, including phone chargers, power banks, headphones, hubs and docks. Anker competes against companies such as Logitech (NASDAQ:LOGI) and Corsair Gaming (NASDAQ:CRSR). Shares for these companies were up 0.5% and down 1%, respectively, during early afternoon trading on Friday. Amazon had edged up about 1%.

Anker said it increased prices due to its bargaining power and the fact that many of its competitors are Chinese firms as well, facing similar tariffs, according to the report.

Amazon CEO Andy Jassy told CNBC on Thursday that many of its third-party sellers will likely increase prices due to the tariffs. Items manufactured in China comprise as much as 70% of the goods sold on Amazon, according to an estimate by Wedbush. Jassy said Amazon has made some "strategic forward inventory buys" and is attempting to renegotiate some terms to alleviate price hikes.

On Friday, China boosted its tariffs on U.S. imports to 125% from 84%, and stated it would disregard any additional tariff hikes announced by Washington moving forward. The White House on Thursday clarified that the cumulative tariff on China-produced goods coming into the U.S. will total 145%.

Earlier in the week, Trump issued a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs on more than 75 countries – excluding China.

Seeking Alpha reached out to Amazon for comment.

data.title: China weighs opening its ETF sector to Western firms - report
data.description:
China has been considering allowing firms such as Jane Street and Citadel Securities to act as market makers in the country's rapidly growing exchange-traded fund sector, according to a media report.

While Chinese regulators have encouraged the development of domestic market makers, international market makers have more experience in providing liquidity to ETFs, Reuters reported on Friday, citing two people with knowledge of the matter.

They also said that such experience would help increase trading efficiency and lower costs. However, the trade war between the U.S. and China could delay the go-ahead for U.S. firms, Reuters reported.

Amsterdam-based market maker Optiver may also benefit from such a shift in Beijing, according to one of the people and a third source.

China's ETF industry more than doubled (+134%) in the past two years to $510B, due to strong inflows from state capital, the article said.

data.title: REITs log $12.2B of capital offerings in Q1 - report
data.description:
REITs gathered $12.2B through secondary debt and equity offerings in Q1, a report by Nareit showed.

A total of $8.9B was raised through debt offerings, $2.6B from common equity offerings, and $700M via preferred equity offerings, the report noted.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase projects $94.5B in firm-wide NII for 2025 amid cautious economic outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jamie Dimon emphasized the firm’s robust performance and readiness to navigate economic uncertainties, citing the banking system's role as a source of strength. He highlighted the firm’s net income of $14.6 billion and EPS of $5.07 on revenue of $46 billion.
  • CFO Jeremy Barnum detailed key financial metrics, including a 21% ROTCE and a $588 million gain related to First Republic. He noted an 8% year-on-year revenue increase and a 4% rise in expenses due to higher compensation and legal costs.
  • The firm reported a $323 million release of the FDIC special assessment accrual, contrasting with a $725 million increase in the prior quarter.
  • The CET1 ratio stood at 15.4%, down 30 basis points due to higher RWA and capital distributions, which included $7.1 billion in net common share repurchases.
  • Dimon also addressed system-wide regulatory challenges, advocating for reforms to foster economic stability and growth.

Outlook

  • The firm maintains its guidance for NII ex-Markets at approximately $90 billion and expects firm-wide NII to reach $94.5 billion, reflecting increased Markets NII.
  • The adjusted expense outlook remains at $95 billion, with the firm reaffirming a card net charge-off rate of around 3.6%.
  • Dimon expressed a cautious stance on the investment banking outlook, citing elevated uncertainty and a wait-and-see sentiment among corporate clients.

Financial Results

  • Revenue reached $46 billion, up $3.5 billion year-on-year, with Markets revenue increasing 21%, driven by strong equities performance.
  • NIR ex-Markets rose 20%, supported by higher asset management fees and investment banking fees.
  • Expenses rose to $23.6 billion, a $840 million increase, largely due to compensation and higher operational costs.
  • Credit costs totaled $3.3 billion, including net charge-offs of $2.3 billion and a net reserve build of $973 million, reflecting adjustments for elevated risks and uncertainties.
  • Consumer and small business financial health remained stable, with spending and credit utilization aligning with expectations. Card outstandings grew 10%, and auto originations rose 20%.

Q&A

  • Ken Usdin, Autonomous: Asked about macroeconomic uncertainty and its impact on growth and reserving expectations. CFO Barnum noted recent soundings and spending patterns, emphasizing a cautious approach due to transitional economic conditions.
  • Erika Najarian, UBS: Queried about economic turbulence and the resilience of banks. Dimon highlighted the sector's financial strength and adaptability but acknowledged potential impacts from a recessionary environment.
  • John McDonald, Truist Securities: Raised concerns on credit card charge-offs amid rising recession risks. Barnum explained that charge-off trajectories are influenced by unemployment trends and are generally predictable in the short term.
  • Betsy Graseck, Morgan Stanley: Discussed deposit trends and loan growth opportunities. Barnum attributed deposit stabilization to reduced yield-seeking behavior and strong client engagement.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious sentiment, emphasizing concerns about macroeconomic uncertainties and potential impacts on credit quality and growth.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone, addressing economic challenges and emphasizing the firm’s preparedness. Jeremy Barnum reiterated confidence in the firm’s risk management and operational strategies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone reflected increased caution, with a focus on navigating potential economic turbulence and regulatory changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased by $2.3 billion from the previous quarter, with Markets revenues and net income reflecting strong growth in equities and client activity.
  • Regulatory capital ratios experienced a slight decline due to higher RWA and focused capital distributions.
  • Management's tone shifted slightly to emphasize caution amid elevated economic uncertainties, contrasting with the more optimistic tone of Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated risks and uncertainties in the macroeconomic environment led to increased probability weightings for downside scenarios.
  • Dimon emphasized potential impacts of regulatory changes and geopolitical issues on the banking sector’s capacity to support economic growth.
  • Analysts raised concerns about credit quality, particularly in consumer and wholesale segments, amid potential recession risks and stagflation scenarios.

Final Takeaway

JPMorgan Chase delivered strong Q1 2025 results, driven by robust performance across Markets and consumer segments, while maintaining a cautious outlook amid economic uncertainties. The firm reaffirmed its guidance for NII and expenses, highlighting its readiness to adapt to evolving market conditions and regulatory environments. Management emphasized the resilience of its financial position and operational capabilities to navigate potential challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Federal spending rises despite DOGE efforts, WSJ finds
data.description:
Federal spending under the Trump administration has risen by $154B compared to the same period in 2024, despite the $150B in savings claimed by the Department of Government Efficiency, according to a Wall Street Journal analysis. 

The increase reflects challenges in curbing costs tied to mandatory programs and interest payments, which account for 73% of federal expenditures. The news outlet noted that DOGE’s efforts to slash contracts and jobs are still ongoing and have yet to make their full impact on the government's overall budget situation.

Mandatory programs like Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid drove much of the spending surge, the Wall Street Journal reported. Social Security payments alone rose $32.7B due to a 2.5% cost-of-living adjustment and 1.3 million new beneficiaries. Medicare and Medicaid spending grew by $29B, while interest on the national debt increased by $25.5B, highlighting structural pressures that outpace DOGE’s cuts.

DOGE’s cost-cutting measures—including terminating 25,000 probationary workers and reducing foreign aid—yielded limited results, the Wall Street Journal found. Small wins included a $4B reduction at the Education Department. 

DOGE was put in place by the Trump administration to curtail government spending and has been operating under the supervision of billionaire businessman Elon Musk. The WSJ's analysis of spending is based on the Treasury Department's daily financial statements.

The Wall Street Journal emphasized that rising interest rates and legal challenges complicate DOGE’s agenda. A Supreme Court ruling reversed paused foreign-aid cuts, and buyout programs may have temporarily increased payroll costs. Analysts told the news outlet that meaningful savings would require years of sustained effort and favorable court outcomes, leaving the long-term impact of DOGE’s initiatives uncertain.

data.title: Sarepta initiated with a bullish view at Wells Fargo on Elevidys
data.description:
Wells Fargo launched its coverage of Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT) with an Overweight recommendation, citing commercial prospects for Elevidys, a gene therapy developed by SRPT with Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) for the hereditary disorder Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

Despite a recent fatal adverse event linked to the drug, “Elevidys remains the best option for most young DMD” patients, and its commercial opportunity remains “largely intact,” Wells Fargo’s Yanan Zhu wrote.

With the adverse event being acute liver failure in a non-ambulatory DMD patient, Wells Fargo, based on expert feedback, noted that liver injury is not an unanticipated outcome related to AAV-mediated gene therapies like Elevidys.

The analyst ruled out a significant impact from the event on the drug’s uptake among ambulatory DMD patients but projected Elevidys use among non-ambulatory patients to be capped at 15%.

However, he issued a $115 per share valuation on the stock, mainly attributed to Elevidys and other drugs in the company’s DMD franchise.

data.title: Fundstrat's Mark Newton says stocks showing signs of bottoming
data.description:
Fundstrat’s global head of technical strategy Mark Newton believes recent market turbulence is showing signs of stabilization after two months of significant damage.

“There is some evidence that equities have in fact made a bottom and/or are in a bottoming process,” he said in an interview with CNBC, pointing to washed-out market breadth and extreme downside volume dispersion.

Newton emphasized that technology stocks and financial sector earnings provided encouraging signals, with the Magnificent Seven showing notable recovery off recent lows. However, he cautioned that any recovery would likely follow an “L-shaped” pattern rather than a V-shaped rebound, predicting sideways movement for 30-60 days before a larger rally emerges. 

"We're likely trying to put in some type of a low," the analyst told the financial news network, but cautioned that "there has been a lot of damage done."

"This is not gonna be a quick move back to new highs," he said. "This is gonna take time."
